outline.heading: Cloning of acrB
outline.preview: # Cloning of acrB acrB was amplified from W3110 chromosomal DNA by PCR using the primers acrB-F and acrBCln-R, which contain the BamHI and SacI restriction sites, respectively. The 3.2-kb PCR fragment was digested with BamHI and SacI and the restricted DNA was cloned into the 4.2-kb vector pMW119 to generate the 7.4-kb plasmid pAcrB. The sequence of the cloned acrB was verified by DNA sequencing.

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Biofuel toxicity limits microbial production yield. Native E. coli efflux pump AcrB can be engineered to improve efflux of biofuel molecules like n-octane. Directed evolution was chosen over rational design due to the dynamic mechanism of AcrB. A competitive growth selection using a toxic surrogate (1-octanol) was developed to enrich for AcrB variants with enhanced efflux, overcoming the lack of high-throughput assay for n-octane.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Directed evolution of AcrB transporter improved efflux of n-octane and α-pinene. A competitive growth selection using a toxic surrogate isolated mutants with up to 47% and 400% increased efflux. Single-site mutants identified N189H, T678S, Q737L, M844L as beneficial. Mutations are distant from substrate channels, highlighting advantage of directed evolution.
process_signals: Problem: biofuel toxicity limits production
Approach: directed evolution of native AcrB
Key result: mutants with improved efflux isolated
Structural insight: beneficial mutations distant from substrate channels
metrics: Efflux efficiency increase: up to 47% for n-octane, 400% for α-pinene

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Biofuel toxicity hinders microbial production. Efflux pumps can mitigate toxicity and aid product recovery. Engineering native AcrB is preferred over heterologous expression to avoid aggregation. Directed evolution chosen because AcrB mechanism is dynamic. n-Octane selected as target; lack of chromophore necessitates growth-based selection. Hypothesis: solvent resistance correlates with growth and efflux rate, enabling enrichment of superior mutants.
process_signals: Motivation: biofuel toxicity and need for efflux engineering
Rationale for native AcrB engineering: avoids heterologous expression issues
Rationale for directed evolution: dynamic mechanism makes rational design difficult
Challenge: n-octane lacks chromophore, no high-throughput assay
Hypothesis: competitive growth under solvent stress enriches for improved efflux mutants
Use of 1-octanol as more toxic surrogate to increase selection pressure
failures_or_limits: n-Octane is mildly toxic, may not provide sufficient selection pressure

section_gists.heading: Competitive growth assay for establishing conditions for library enrichment
section_gists.gist: Established selection conditions: n-octane at basal AcrB expression, 1-octanol at 0.050% with IPTG induction. Demonstrated that selection allows enrichment of tolerant cells. Control experiment showed plasmid shift from mixed to only pAcrB under selection.
process_signals: Initial step: determine hydrocarbon tolerance of E. coli strains
Used acrB knockout strain JA300A with plasmid complementation
n-Octane selection: basal expression to apply adequate pressure
1-Octanol selection: 0.050% with induction for stronger pressure
Validation: mixed culture experiment showed enrichment of pAcrB under selection
methods: Growth rate measurement under hydrocarbon stress
Competitive growth assay with mixed strains

section_gists.heading: Efflux rate determination of n-octane
section_gists.gist: Developed a discontinuous GC-based assay to measure n-octane efflux. Cells loaded with n-octane after AcrB inactivation, then efflux measured over time. Efflux rate constant calculated as difference between strains with and without AcrB.
process_signals: Challenge: no high-throughput method for n-octane
Solution: discontinuous assay using GC quantification
Protocol: inactivate AcrB with CCCP, load n-octane, reactivate with glucose, measure intracellular n-octane over time
Data analysis: first-order kinetics, rate constant difference
failures_or_limits: Low throughput due to discontinuous assay

section_gists.heading: Growth enrichment of mutant libraries and selection based on determination of efflux rates
section_gists.gist: Eight mutant libraries (~20,000 variants each, 3 mutations/kb) generated by error-prone PCR. Enrichment initially with n-octane, then switched to 1-octanol due to insufficient selection pressure. Enriched libraries screened as pools by efflux assay; best pool (Library 1) selected. Individual clones isolated; 1G1 and 1G2 showed 47% and 44% increased n-octane efflux. Sequencing revealed 7 mutations. Single-site mutants constructed; N189H, T678S, Q737L, M844L beneficial. T678A also improved efflux. 1G1 and 1G2 also improved α-pinene efflux (400% and 250%).
process_signals: Library generation: error-prone PCR, 8 libraries
Initial enrichment with n-octane, but insufficient pressure
Switch to 1-octanol enrichment for stronger selection
Screening strategy: assay enriched pools first, then isolate clones
Identification of 1G1 and 1G2 with ~45% improvement
Sequencing: 1G1 has N189H, T678S, Q737L; 1G2 has L357Q, M844L, M987T, V1028M
Single-site analysis: N189H and T678S individually small effect, suggest synergy
T678A also beneficial, indicating importance of residue 678
Cross-substrate testing: mutants also improve α-pinene efflux
methods: Competitive growth enrichment with n-octane and 1-octanol
Single clone isolation and efflux assay
GC-based efflux assay for n-octane and α-pinene
metrics: Efflux rate constants and relative rates
Fold improvement: n-octane 1.47x, α-pinene up to 4x
failures_or_limits: n-Octane enrichment insufficient; had to switch to 1-octanol
Some single-site mutants (L357Q, M987T, V1028M) reduced efflux
N189H and T678S individually gave <10% improvement, but combined in 1G1 gave 47%

section_gists.heading: Characterization of AcrB variants and structural analysis
section_gists.gist: 1G1 and 1G2 showed faster growth in 1-octanol and increased n-octane efflux. Western blot confirmed similar expression levels, so improvement due to protein function. Structural mapping: L357Q, M987T, V1028M in transmembrane domain, likely detrimental. N189H and Q737L near TolC docking domains, may improve AcrB-TolC assembly. T678S and M844L near substrate entrance; T678S may widen entrance, M844L may enlarge substrate path. Mutations distant from known channels, validating directed evolution approach.
process_signals: Validation: growth and efflux improvement confirmed for 1G1 and 1G2
Expression level control: western blot shows no overexpression
Structural analysis using PDB 2DHH (asymmetric structure)
Detrimental mutations in transmembrane domain
Beneficial mutations in periplasmic domain: TolC docking and substrate path
Mechanistic hypotheses: N189H/Q737L aid TolC assembly; T678S widens entrance; M844L enlarges path
Advantage of directed evolution: mutations distant from known channels
methods: Structural mapping on AcrB crystal structure
failures_or_limits: Exact mechanism of N189H and Q737L unclear
Structural analysis based on existing crystal structure, not new experimental structure

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Competitive growth selection using 1-octanol successfully isolated AcrB mutants with improved n-octane efflux. Directed evolution identified non-obvious mutations distant from substrate path. Mutations may improve efflux by enlarging path, altering dynamics, or facilitating TolC assembly. Strategy useful for low-toxicity biofuels. Next step: validate in biofuel-producing strains.
process_signals: Success of competitive growth selection with surrogate
Directed evolution found unexpected beneficial mutations
Proposed mechanisms: path enlargement, dynamics, TolC assembly
Future work: test in biofuel-producing strains
failures_or_limits: Need to validate in actual biofuel production systems

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial hypothesis: solvent resistance correlates with efflux rate, enabling growth-based selection.
n-Octane alone insufficient for selection, leading to use of more toxic surrogate 1-octanol.
Directed evolution can identify non-obvious mutations that rational design would miss.
Beneficial mutations may act synergistically (N189H and T678S).
Mutations improve efflux by multiple mechanisms: substrate path widening, TolC assembly, dynamics.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact mechanism of N189H and Q737L in improving efflux.
Whether synergistic effects of N189H and T678S can be confirmed by double mutant.
Performance of mutants in actual biofuel-producing strains.
Generalizability to other biofuel molecules beyond n-octane and α-pinene.

evidence_bank.quote: n-octane has no chromophore, thus efflux rate of n-octane from E. coli cannot be conveniently determined spectroscopically in real time.

Based on the hypothesis that solvent resistance correlates to increased growth and efflux rate, subjecting E. coli cells expressing a mixture of AcrB variants to the toxicity of n-octane would enable the superior mutants to dominate the population

competitive growth enrichment of the mutant libraries with n-octane would be performed at basal expression level. Under this condition, the growth rate of cells with wild-type AcrB n-octane efflux efficiency is sufficiently lower than the maximum growth rate achievable by the cells.
competitive growth enrichment of the mutant libraries with 1-octanol was performed using induced cells with 0.050% of the substrate.
Plasmids isolated from the control cultures consisted of both pMW119 and pAcrB while those extracted from cultures grown under the selection pressure of the hydrocarbons contained only pAcrB
the n-octane efflux rate was determined by a discontinuous assay using gas chromatography to quantify the intracellular n-octane over time

The efflux rate of n-octane by AcrB was calculated by taking the difference between the n-octane release rate constants of JA300A/pMW119 (expressing no AcrB) and JA300A/pAcrB (expressing wild-type AcrB)
Eight mutant libraries of acrB were generated by mutagenic PCR and cloned into pMW119. Each library consisted of approximately 20,000 variants
Library enrichment was initially performed using n-octane.
n-octane could not apply sufficient selection pressure to enable differentiation of variants with good performance from the average ones.
Sequencing of five discrete clones isolated from a library revealed mutations in the lac operator in four clones
the libraries were dominated by plasmids with mutant lac operator expressing AcrB variants with average performance
To overcome this limitation, we directed our effort towards growth enrichment using 1-octanol.
AcrB expression was induced with IPTG before and during growth enrichment with 1-octanol
Two of the eight enriched libraries contained variants with enhanced n-octane efflux efficiency.
The mutations in 1G1 are N189H and T678S, and those in 1G2 are L357Q, Q737L, M844L, M987T, V1028M.
The variants also exhibited over 40% increase in n-octane efflux rate relative to wild-type AcrB
There was negligible difference in the expression levels of 1G1 and 1G2 compared to wild-type AcrB
indicating that the increased efflux of n-octane and enhanced tolerance of the cells expressing 1G1 and 1G2 were due to improved performance of the mutant proteins.
N189H and T678S improved efflux of n-octane only slightly (less than 10%) although 1G1, which possess these two mutations, exhibited 47% faster efflux of n-octane.
These results suggest that N189H and T678S may work synergistically.
N189H and Q737L are located near the hairpins in the TolC docking domains
T678S and M844L occur close to each other along two proposed transport paths i.e. the vestibule between protomers and the cleft in the periplasmic domain
R717 and L828 such that the channel leading to the binding pocket is enlarged or the dynamics of substrate efflux is altered.
these propositions on the roles of the T678S and M844L mutations are supported by the increased n-octane efflux by the T678A and M844A variants
the beneficial effects of the mutations are not specific to 1-octanol and n-octane only, but may be generally important for improving efflux of small hydrophobic hydrocarbon molecules.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Biofuel toxicity limits microbial production yield; engineering native E. coli efflux pump AcrB via directed evolution to improve efflux of biofuel molecules like n-octane.

canonical_events.summary: n-Octane lacks chromophore, preventing high-throughput spectroscopic efflux assay.
Hypothesis that solvent resistance correlates with growth and efflux rate, enabling competitive growth selection.
Design of competitive growth selection using n-octane at basal AcrB expression to apply adequate pressure.
Design of competitive growth selection using 1-octanol as more toxic surrogate with IPTG induction.
Validation of selection conditions: mixed culture of JA300A/pMW119 and JA300A/pAcrB enriched to only pAcrB under hydrocarbon stress.
Development of discontinuous GC-based assay to measure n-octane efflux rate.
Efflux rate constant calculated as difference between n-octane release rate constants of strains with and without AcrB.
Generation of eight mutant libraries (~20,000 variants each) by error-prone PCR.

Library enrichment initially performed using n-octane, but n-octane could not apply sufficient selection pressure; enriched libraries dominated by plasmids with mutant lac operator, not improved AcrB mutants.
Switched to growth enrichment using 1-octanol to overcome insufficient selection pressure.
Two enriched libraries showed enhanced n-octane efflux; variants 1G1 and 1G2 isolated with mutations N189H, T678S, L357Q, Q737L, M844L, M987T, V1028M.
1G1 and 1G2 showed faster growth in 1-octanol and over 40% increase in n-octane efflux; western blot confirmed similar expression levels, so improvement due to protein function.
Single-site mutants revealed N189H and T678S may work synergistically (individually <10% improvement, together 47%); Q737L and M844L beneficial; L357Q, M987T, V1028M reduced efflux.
Structural mapping: N189H and Q737L near TolC docking domains; T678S and M844L near substrate entrance; mutations distant from substrate path validate directed evolution approach.
Hypothesized that T678S and M844L mutations enlarge substrate channel or alter efflux dynamics, supported by T678A and M844A variants.
Investigated antibiotic resistance of mutants and found no increased resistance to nalidixic acid, chloramphenicol, or tetracycline.

Structural mapping provides mechanistic insights into the mutations.

Antibiotic resistance testing is a further characterization step.
Results of antibiotic testing lead to reasoning about safety.
Structural analysis validates the advantage of directed evolution.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution of AcrB yielded variants 1G1 and 1G2 with over 40% increased n-octane efflux. Beneficial mutations N189H, T678S, Q737L, M844L were identified, with N189H and T678S showing synergy. Mutations are distant from the substrate path, validating the directed evolution approach. Sequencing of five discrete clones isolated from a library revealed mutations in the lac operator in four clones
the libraries were dominated by plasmids with mutant lac operator expressing AcrB variants with average performance

evolution_trajectory.state_before: n-Octane enrichment failed due to insufficient selection pressure.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a more stringent selection to isolate improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: 1-Octanol with IPTG induction will provide stronger selection pressure.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Switch to growth enrichment using 1-octanol with IPTG induction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Enrichment strategy revised to use 1-octanol.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform 1-octanol enrichment and screen for improved variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: To overcome this limitation, we directed our effort towards growth enrichment using 1-octanol.
AcrB expression was induced with IPTG before and during growth enrichment with 1-octanol

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Revised selection strategy using 1-octanol.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether 1-octanol enrichment can isolate improved AcrB variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: 1-Octanol enrichment will yield variants with enhanced n-octane efflux.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Enrich libraries with 1-octanol and isolate individual variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two enriched libraries showed enhanced n-octane efflux; variants 1G1 (N189H, T678S) and 1G2 (L357Q, Q737L, M844L, M987T, V1028M) isolated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate improved growth and efflux of 1G1 and 1G2.

evidence.quote_or_span: Two of the eight enriched libraries contained variants with enhanced n-octane efflux efficiency.
The mutations in 1G1 are N189H and T678S, and those in 1G2 are L357Q, Q737L, M844L, M987T, V1028M.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Variants 1G1 and 1G2 isolated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the improved growth and efflux are due to protein function.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: 1G1 and 1G2 will show faster growth in 1-octanol and increased n-octane efflux; similar expression levels indicate functional improvement.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize growth, efflux, and expression of 1G1 and 1G2.

tool_or_method.name: growth assay, GC efflux assay, western blot

evolution_trajectory.observation: 1G1 and 1G2 showed faster growth in 1-octanol and over 40% increase in n-octane efflux; western blot confirmed similar expression levels.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze contributions of individual mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The variants also exhibited over 40% increase in n-octane efflux rate relative to wild-type AcrB
There was negligible difference in the expression levels of 1G1 and 1G2 compared to wild-type AcrB
indicating that the increased efflux of n-octane and enhanced tolerance of the cells expressing 1G1 and 1G2 were due to improved performance of the mutant proteins.
The proteins were transferred to polyvinylidene fluoride membranes (Bio-Rad) electrophoretically and probed using anti-hexahistidine antibody conjugated with horse radish peroxidase.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 1G1 and 1G2 validated with improved efflux.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutations are responsible for the improved phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Single-site mutants will reveal contributions and potential synergy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct and assay single-site mutants for all mutations in 1G1 and 1G2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: N189H and T678S individually <10% improvement, but together 47%; Q737L and M844L beneficial; L357Q, M987T, V1028M reduced efflux. Synergy between N189H and T678S.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Map mutations onto AcrB structure to understand mechanism.

evidence.quote_or_span: N189H and T678S improved efflux of n-octane only slightly (less than 10%) although 1G1, which possess these two mutations, exhibited 47% faster efflux of n-octane.
These results suggest that N189H and T678S may work synergistically.

Single-site mutants based on the mutations found in the isolated variants were synthesized and the amino acid substitutions N189H, T678S, Q737L and M844L were identified to have conferred improvement in efflux efficiency.
Interestingly, N189H and T678S improved efflux of n-octane only slightly (less than 10%) although 1G1, which possess these two mutations, exhibited 47% faster efflux of n-octane.
The mutations in 1G1 are N189H and T678S, and those in 1G2 are L357Q, Q737L, M844L, M987T, V1028M.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Key beneficial mutations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Structural basis for improved efflux.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations are located in functionally relevant regions: N189H and Q737L near TolC docking domains; T678S and M844L near substrate entrance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Map mutations onto AcrB crystal structure (PDB: 2DHH).
evolution_trajectory.observation: N189H and Q737L near TolC docking hairpins; T678S and M844L close to each other along proposed transport paths. Mutations distant from substrate path validate directed evolution approach.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Propose mechanistic hypotheses for T678S and M844L.

evidence.quote_or_span: N189H and Q737L are located near the hairpins in the TolC docking domains
T678S and M844L occur close to each other along two proposed transport paths i.e. the vestibule between protomers and the cleft in the periplasmic domain
The locations of the mutations were studied on a published structure of AcrB (PDB ID: 2DHH).
Rendered images of protein structures were generated with Pymol [33].

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mechanism of T678S and M844L improvement.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test hypothesis with T678A and M844A variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: T678A and M844A also increased n-octane efflux, supporting channel enlargement hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate antibiotic resistance of mutants.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution of AcrB yielded variants 1G1 and 1G2 with over 40% increased n-octane efflux. Beneficial mutations N189H, T678S, Q737L, M844L were identified, with N189H and T678S showing synergy. Mutations are distant from the substrate path, validating the directed evolution approach. No increased antibiotic resistance was observed.
limitations: Crystal structures would be required to validate mechanistic hypotheses (EV_C003_E07_01).
